docs.title: 📌 Executive Summary
docs.text:
This portal defines the enterprise-grade security and secrets management platform for Openserve applications using Azure Key Vault, Managed Identity, and RBAC governance.

It establishes a unified, identity-driven security model that eliminates static credentials, enforces Zero Trust principles, and introduces full operational governance across all environments.

docs.title: 🎯 Business Outcomes
docs.text:
This platform delivers:

  • 🔐 Reduced credential exposure and leakage risk
  • ⚙️ Centralized secrets management across all environments
  • 📊 Improved compliance alignment (Zero Trust architecture)
  • 🏛 Enforced governance and access control model
  • 🔄 Controlled and auditable secret lifecycle management
  • 🧩 Strong environment isolation (Dev / Non-Prod / Prod)

docs.title: 🧭 Portal Scope
docs.text:
This documentation covers the full lifecycle of Azure Key Vault adoption:

  • 🏗 Architecture design and security model
  • 🏛 Governance and access control framework
  • 🧪 Proof of Concept validation
  • 🚀 Migration and operational runbooks
  • 📋 Deployment checklists and standards

docs.title: 🏛 Platform Architecture Principle
docs.text:
All applications retrieve secrets at runtime using identity-based authentication:

Application → Managed Identity / Service Principal → Azure Key Vault → Secure Runtime Injection

docs.title: 🔐 Security & Governance Model
docs.text:
The platform enforces:

  • Authentication: Managed Identity (preferred), Service Principal (external systems only)
  • Authorization: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
  • Governance: Controlled ownership, approvals, and change management
  • Auditability: Azure Monitor logging and traceability of all access
  • Secret Handling: Runtime-only usage (no persistence, no .env files)

docs.title: 🧭 Platform Domains
docs.text: Domain Purpose 🏗 Architecture System design and integration model 🏛 Governance Ownership, access control, and compliance 🧪 POC Technical validation and proof of implementation 🚀 Runbooks Operational procedures and migrations 📋 Checklists Deployment readiness and validation

docs.title: 📚 Navigation
docs.text:
• 🏗 Architecture
  • 🏛 Governance
  • 🧪 Proof of Concept
  • 🚀 Runbooks
  • 📋 Deployment Checklist

docs.title: ⚙️ Design Principles
docs.text:
• Zero Trust Security Model
  • Identity-Based Access Control
  • Infrastructure Decoupling
  • Environment Isolation
  • Full Auditability and Traceability
  • No Static Credentials

docs.title: 🧠 Why This Matters
docs.text:
This platform replaces insecure .env-based secret management with a cloud-native, identity-driven security and governance model, enabling:

  • Safer deployments
  • Stronger compliance posture
  • Reduced operational risk
  • Enterprise-grade control over secrets lifecycle

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
This document defines the enterprise architecture for secure secret management using Azure Key Vault across Openserve application environments.

It establishes a standardized, identity-driven security model for secret storage, access control, and runtime retrieval using Azure-native services.

docs.title: 2. Objectives
docs.text:
• Centralize all application secrets in Azure Key Vault
  • Eliminate .env files and embedded credentials
  • Enable secure runtime secret retrieval
  • Enforce identity-based access control (Zero Trust model)
  • Provide full auditability of secret access across environments

docs.title: 3. Architecture Principles
docs.text:
• Zero Trust Security Model: No static credentials in application code
  • Identity-Based Access: Managed Identity preferred over Service Principal
  • Separation of Concerns: Applications, secrets, and infrastructure are decoupled
  • Environment Isolation: Separate Key Vault per environment (Dev / Non-Prod / Prod)

docs.title: Logical Flow
docs.text: Application → Managed Identity → Azure Key Vault → Secure Secret Retrieval → Runtime Injection

docs.title: Azure Key Vault
docs.text:
Centralized secure store for:

  • Database credentials
  • API keys
  • Authentication secrets
  • Certificates and cryptographic material

docs.title: Azure App Service
docs.text:
• Hosts application workloads
  • Uses Managed Identity for authentication
  • Retrieves secrets at runtime securely

docs.title: Managed Identity
docs.text:
• System-managed Azure identity
  • Eliminates need for stored credentials
  • Authenticates applications to Azure services securely

docs.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text:
• Assigns Key Vault Secrets User role
  • Enforces least-privilege access model
  • Restricts secret access to authorized identities only

docs.title: Authentication Strategy
docs.text: Scenario Authentication Method Rationale Azure-hosted workloads (App Service, Functions, Containers) Managed Identity Secure, no credentials required External systems (CI/CD, local development, on-prem systems) Service Principal Required for non-Azure authentication

docs.title: 7. Runtime Data Flow
docs.text:
1. Application starts in Azure App Service
  2. Managed Identity is automatically assigned
  3. Application requests secret from Azure Key Vault
  4. Azure validates identity and RBAC permissions
  5. Secret is securely returned at runtime
  6. Secret is used in memory only (no persistence)

docs.title: 8. Security Controls
docs.text:
• No secrets stored in source code or repositories
  • No .env files deployed to any environment
  • RBAC enforced at Key Vault level
  • All access logged via Azure Monitor (audit trail)
  • Secret rotation supported without application redeployment

docs.title: 9. Environment Strategy
docs.text:
Each environment is fully isolated:

  • Development → Feature development and testing
  • Non-Production → Integration and validation testing
  • Production → Live customer workloads

Each environment includes: - Dedicated Key Vault instance - Independent identity assignments - Isolated secret namespaces

docs.title: 10. Benefits
docs.text:
This architecture delivers:

  • 🔐 Reduced credential exposure risk
  • ⚙️ Centralized and consistent secret management
  • 📊 Improved compliance posture (Zero Trust alignment)
  • 🔄 Simplified secret rotation lifecycle
  • 👁️ Full audit visibility of secret access

docs.title: 11. Operational Considerations
docs.text:
• RBAC changes may take time to propagate
  • Application restart may be required after secret updates
  • Key Vault reference strings are case-sensitive
  • Misconfiguration results in runtime resolution failures

docs.title: 12. Conclusion
docs.text:
Azure Key Vault enables a secure, scalable, and enterprise-grade secrets management architecture based on identity and Zero Trust principles.

This eliminates dependency on insecure configuration patterns such as .env files and ensures consistent, auditable secret handling across all environments.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text: Ensure all deployments using Azure Key Vault are secure, validated, and production-ready.

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text: Applies to Dev, Non-Prod, and Production environments.

docs.title: 3. Source Control Security
docs.text:
• [ ] No .env or secret files in repository
  • [ ] .gitignore and .dockerignore properly configured
  • [ ] No secrets in code, configs, or pipelines

docs.title: 4. Key Vault & Identity
docs.text:
• [ ] Key Vault configured per environment
  • [ ] Secrets created and verified
  • [ ] Managed Identity enabled
  • [ ] Least-privilege access assigned (RBAC)

docs.title: 5. Application Configuration
docs.text:
• [ ] No environment file dependency
  • [ ] No hardcoded secrets
  • [ ] Secrets resolved at runtime via Key Vault
  • [ ] Configuration is environment-agnostic

docs.title: 6. Build & Deployment
docs.text:
• [ ] Build succeeds
  • [ ] CI/CD pipeline completes without errors
  • [ ] Deployment contains no secrets
  • [ ] Application restarts successfully

docs.title: 7. Runtime Validation
docs.text:
• [ ] Application starts successfully
  • [ ] Authentication to Key Vault succeeds
  • [ ] Secrets resolve correctly
  • [ ] Database and external integrations work
  • [ ] No auth or runtime errors

docs.title: 8. Monitoring & Logging
docs.text:
• [ ] Monitoring enabled
  • [ ] Key Vault access logs available
  • [ ] No secret leakage in logs
  • [ ] No repeated access failures

docs.title: 9. Security Controls
docs.text:
• [ ] RBAC correctly enforced
  • [ ] Least privilege applied
  • [ ] No secrets stored outside Key Vault references
  • [ ] Zero Trust principles maintained

docs.title: 10. Rollback Readiness
docs.text:
• [ ] Previous configuration securely stored
  • [ ] Rollback steps documented
  • [ ] Recovery path validated
  • [ ] System can safely revert to last stable state

docs.title: 11. Deployment Approval
docs.text:
Deployment is only approved if:

  • All checklist items are completed
  • No critical risks remain
  • Runbook has been followed
  • Governance requirements are satisfied

docs.title: 1. Objective
docs.text: Validate that Azure App Service can securely retrieve secrets from Azure Key Vault using Managed Identity, eliminating the need for .env files and embedded credentials.

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
This Proof of Concept validates the following capabilities:

  • Secure storage of secrets in Azure Key Vault
  • App Service integration using Key Vault references
  • Managed Identity authentication
  • Runtime secret resolution at application startup
  • RBAC-based access control enforcement

docs.title: Out of Scope
docs.text:
• Secret rotation automation
  • Multi-region redundancy
  • CI/CD pipeline integration

docs.title: 3. Architecture Overview
docs.text: Application → Managed Identity → Azure Key Vault → Secure Secret Retrieval → Runtime Injection

docs.title: 4.1 Key Vault Configuration
docs.text:
Azure Key Vault instance created and configured with required secrets:

  • DATABASE_PASSWORD (database authentication)
  • AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET (service authentication)
  • API_GATEWAY_SUBSCRIPTION_KEY (external API access)

docs.title: 4.2 App Service Configuration
docs.text:
• System Assigned Managed Identity enabled
  • Role assigned: Key Vault Secrets User

Key Vault reference used in App Settings:

@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://.vault.azure.net/secrets//)

docs.title: 5. Key Behaviour
docs.text:
• Secrets are resolved at runtime by Azure App Service
  • No .env files are used in deployment
  • No hardcoded credentials exist in codebase
  • Secrets are never persisted locally or in logs

docs.title: 6. Validation Results
docs.text:
• Application starts successfully
  • Managed Identity authentication succeeds
  • Secrets resolve correctly from Key Vault
  • Database connection established
  • External API authentication successful
  • No fallback to environment variables detected

docs.title: 7. Conclusion
docs.text: This Proof of Concept confirms that Azure Key Vault with Managed Identity provides a secure, production-ready replacement for traditional .env-based secret management.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This runbook defines the standardized process for migrating application secrets from: - .env files - App Service Configuration - Embedded credentials

to Azure Key Vault using Managed Identity and RBAC.

docs.title: 2. Scope
docs.text:
All environments:

  • Development
  • Non-Prod
  • Production

docs.title: 3. Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting migration:

  • Azure Key Vault created per environment
  • Required secrets already stored in Key Vault:
  • DATABASE-PASSWORD
  • CLIENT-SECRET
  • API-SUBSCRIPTION-KEY
  • App Service configured with System Assigned Managed Identity
  • RBAC role assigned: Key Vault Secrets User
  • Application deployment access available

docs.title: Identity Check
docs.text:
• Confirm Managed Identity is enabled on App Service
  • Confirm identity exists in Azure AD

docs.title: Key Vault Access Check
docs.text:
• Validate App Service has Key Vault Secrets User role
  • Confirm Key Vault is reachable from App Service

docs.title: Secret Availability
docs.text:
• Ensure all required secrets exist in Key Vault
  • Confirm secret names match application expectations (case-sensitive)

docs.title: Source Control Security Check
docs.text: Ensure repository does NOT contain exposed secrets:

docs.title: Verify .gitignore
docs.text: Must include: - .env - .env.* - *.env

docs.title: Verify .dockerignore
docs.text: Must include: - .env - .env.*

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: • No secrets must exist in source control or Docker build context

docs.title: Step 1: Create or Verify Secrets in Key Vault
docs.text: Ensure all required secrets exist in Azure Key Vault.

docs.title: Step 2: Enable Managed Identity
docs.text: In Azure Portal: - Navigate to App Service - Go to Identity - Enable System Assigned Identity - Save changes

docs.title: Step 3: Assign RBAC Permissions
docs.text:
Assign role:

  • Key Vault Secrets User

Scope: - Target Key Vault only

docs.title: Step 4: Update Application Configuration
docs.text:
Replace App Settings values with Key Vault references:

@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://.vault.azure.net/secrets//)

docs.title: 🏛 Security & Governance Model – Azure Key Vault Platform
1. Overview
docs.text:
This document defines the governance model for managing Azure Key Vault within the Openserve platform.

It establishes ownership, control, access management, risk handling, and operational accountability for all secrets.

This ensures the platform is not only technically secure, but also audit-ready and enterprise-governed.

docs.title: 2. Governance Objectives
docs.text:
• Define clear ownership of Key Vault resources
  • Enforce controlled access to secrets
  • Establish change management process for secret updates
  • Ensure compliance with security and audit requirements
  • Reduce operational and security risk exposure

docs.title: 3. Ownership Model
docs.text: Component Owner Responsibility Azure Key Vault Platform Engineering Team Provisioning, configuration, lifecycle Secrets Application Team Creation and maintenance RBAC Access Platform Security Team Approval and enforcement App Service Integration DevOps Team Deployment and configuration

docs.title: 4.1 Principle
docs.text: Access is granted based on least privilege and explicit approval only.

docs.title: 4.2 Access Control Model
docs.text:
• Managed Identity (preferred for Azure workloads)
  • Service Principal (only for external systems)

docs.title: 4.3 Approval Requirement
docs.text:
All access requests must include:

  • Business justification
  • Environment scope (Dev / Non-Prod / Prod)
  • Duration of access (temporary preferred)

docs.title: 5. Change Management Process
docs.text:
All changes to secrets must follow this process:

  1. Request submitted by application team
  2. Reviewed by platform engineering
  3. Approved by security owner
  4. Change implemented in Key Vault
  5. Application updated (if required)
  6. Change logged for audit purposes

docs.title: 6.1 Creation
docs.text:
• Secrets must be created in Azure Key Vault only
  • No secrets allowed in .env files or source code

docs.title: 6.2 Rotation
docs.text:
• Secrets should be rotated periodically (based on risk profile)
  • Rotation must not require application redeployment (where possible)

docs.title: 6.3 Decommissioning
docs.text:
• Old secrets must be disabled before deletion
  • Removal must be logged and approved

docs.title: 7.1 Identified Risks
docs.text: Risk Impact Mitigation Key Vault outage Application failure Retry policies + fallback strategy Incorrect RBAC configuration Access failure (403 errors) Role validation checklist Secret misconfiguration Runtime failure POC validation + checklist enforcement

docs.title: 8. Audit & Compliance
docs.text:
• All Key Vault access is logged via Azure Monitor
  • RBAC changes are tracked and auditable
  • Secret access is traceable per identity
  • Supports compliance requirements (SOC2 / ISO27001 aligned principles)

docs.title: Platform Engineering Team
docs.text:
• Infrastructure provisioning
  • RBAC configuration
  • Key Vault lifecycle management

docs.title: DevOps Team
docs.text:
• App Service integration
  • Deployment configuration
  • Runtime validation

docs.title: Security Team
docs.text:
• Access approval
  • Compliance enforcement
  • Audit review

docs.title: 10. Governance Principles
docs.text:
• No shared credentials
  • No unmanaged secret storage
  • No direct human access to production secrets
  • All access must be identity-based
  • All changes must be auditable

docs.title: 11. Conclusion
docs.text:
This governance model ensures Azure Key Vault is not only technically implemented, but also operationally controlled, secure, and audit-compliant.

It completes the enterprise architecture by adding ownership, accountability, and control mechanisms over all secrets in the platform.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
This guide provides standardized troubleshooting procedures for Azure Key Vault integration issues across App Service environments using Managed Identity and Key Vault references.

It enables rapid diagnosis and resolution of authentication, configuration, and runtime failures.

docs.title: Error
docs.text: { "code": 403, "status": "Forbidden", "errorCode": "InvalidAuthorization", "errorMsg": "An invalid signature was specified in the Authorization header" }

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: Authentication to Azure Key Vault failed due to an invalid signature.

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
• Managed Identity not enabled
  • Missing RBAC role assignment
  • RBAC propagation delay
  • Incorrect Key Vault reference format

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Enable Managed Identity on App Service
  • Assign role: Key Vault Secrets User
  • Verify reference format:

@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://.vault.azure.net/secrets//)

  • Restart App Service
  • Wait 5–10 minutes for RBAC propagation

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• Assign RBAC before deployment
  • Avoid restarting immediately after permission changes

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: NOAUTH Authentication required WRONGPASS invalid username-password pair at parseError (/node_modules/redis-parser/lib/parser.js)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: Authentication failed. The client may throw parser-level errors due to malformed AUTH input.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• For Instance Redis connects but commands fail
  • Repeated NOAUTH / WRONGPASS
  • Logs reference redis-parser or parseError

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
• Key Vault secret not resolved
  • Secret URI passed instead of actual value
  • Invalid credential format
  • App initialized before secrets load

docs.title: Critical Indicator
docs.text:
If logs show:

auth: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets//

👉 This confirms Key Vault URI is being passed instead of the secret value

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Ensure Key Vault reference resolves correctly
  • Confirm application receives actual secret value
  • Validate Redis config binding
  • Ensure secrets load before Redis initialization
  • Restart App Service

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• Never pass Key Vault URI to Redis
  • Add startup validation for secrets
  • Ensure proper initialization order

docs.title: Example
docs.text: @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=...)

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: App Service is not resolving the Key Vault reference.

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
• Managed Identity not enabled
  • Missing RBAC permissions
  • Incorrect SecretUri format
  • Key Vault network restrictions

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Enable Managed Identity
  • Assign RBAC role
  • Verify SecretUri format
  • Check firewall / private endpoint settings
  • Restart App Service

docs.title: Error
docs.text: { "code": 403, "status": "Forbidden", "errorCode": "InvalidAuthorization", "errorMsg": "An invalid signature was specified in the Authorization header" }

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: Request to Azure API Management (APIM) failed due to an invalid or malformed Authorization header.

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text:
• HTTP 403 from API calls
  • Request fails before backend
  • No backend logs triggered

docs.title: Common Causes
docs.text:
• Incorrect API key / subscription key
  • Malformed or expired token
  • Wrong Authorization header format
  • Key Vault secret not resolved
  • Key Vault URI passed instead of actual value

docs.title: Critical Pattern
docs.text:
Authorization: https://.vault.azure.net/secrets/...

👉 Indicates Key Vault URI used instead of actual token

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Verify correct header format:
  • Authorization: Bearer <token>
  • Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key: <key>
  • Confirm secret resolves from Key Vault
  • Validate token generation
  • Remove whitespace / encoding issues
  • Restart application

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• Never inject Key Vault URI into headers
  • Validate headers before sending requests
  • Add safe logging for header structure

docs.title: Meaning
docs.text: Access to Key Vault is denied.

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
• Missing RBAC role
  • Incorrect role scope
  • Access policy conflicts

docs.title: Resolution
docs.text:
• Assign correct RBAC role at Key Vault scope
  • Remove conflicting policies
  • Wait for propagation
  • Restart application

docs.title: 7. Error Classification Matrix
docs.text: Layer Error Type Example Key Vault Identity / RBAC InvalidAuthorization Redis Authentication NOAUTH / WRONGPASS Redis Client Parser parseError APIM Header / Auth Format InvalidAuthorization Key Vault Access Control 403 Forbidden

docs.title: 8. Quick Diagnostic Checklist
docs.text:
Before escalating, confirm:

  • Managed Identity is enabled
  • RBAC role assigned correctly
  • Key Vault reference syntax is valid
  • Secrets exist in Key Vault
  • Application restarted after changes
  • No Key Vault network restrictions

docs.title: 9. Troubleshooting Flow (Recommended Order)
docs.text:
1. Identity → Managed Identity enabled
  2. Access → RBAC roles assigned
  3. Configuration → Key Vault reference format
  4. Runtime → Secret resolution and usage

docs.title: 10. Cross References
docs.text:
• Architecture → architecture/key-vault.md
  • POC → poc/key-vault-poc.md
  • Runbook → runbook/key-vault-migration.md
  • Checklist → checklist/deployment-checklist.md

docs.title: 11. Conclusion
docs.text:
Most issues originate from:

  • Identity misconfiguration
  • RBAC permission gaps
  • Incorrect secret resolution

Following a structured diagnostic approach ensures fast and consistent resolution.
